_CURRENT AFFAIRS,

There is a paper called 7'he Irish Vintner and Grocer.
Its March number contained some scurrilous references
to a gentleman of proved ability and character, who is

_ an occasional and welcome contributor to our own
 columns. This journal swaggered like Fulstaff in March,
and talked like a fish-wife -at Mr. Richard Hazel-
ton; in April this one-time swaggering organ
hines like a whipped spaniel at the feet of Mr. Hazel-

In 4 note in the March number of this Irish

and Grocer we read :—* Talking of 8t. Patrick’s

am reminded of a young man named Hazelton,

10 lives in Blackrock, and who has associated himself

lie movement for the closing of licensed houses in

connection with the celebration of the National Holiday.
In a recent number of Tur LeabER—a congenial atmos-
phere for men like Mr. Hazelton—he spread himself
over a column and a-half or so of the paper reviling the
un-national spirit of such licensed traders as would carry
on business on that day, and threatening in a Simon
Tappertit fashion the terrible bolts of an outraged public
sentiment on the heads of such anti-Irish Irishmen.
This day, he asserts, should be a general holiday, a day
in which there would be universal suspension of business
of all kinds, and when we should sit down and think and
do nothing else. The licensed trade of
Dublin will be excused from taking seriously the attacks
of a mooncalf whose only claim to authority as an ex-
ponent of public sentiment as to what the publicans
should do on St. Patrick’s Day would seem to be that he
himself has a holiday all the year round. I should vex
myself were I to pursue the subject any farther, but
really it is most humiliating to think that when this irre-
sponsible chap screws up his handsome mouth and says
things ridiculous, a certain section of the public can’t
see that they have not the real article. The bulk of the
licensed traders of Dublin have to work very hard all the
twelve calendar months of the year to make an honest
living and pay their way, and here is a picture of a
young man, who I understand does nothing at all for a
living, dictating to those men as to what they should do
on St. Patrick’s Day. It is enough to make honest-
minded men lose all hope and trust in an Irish Revival
when they see associated with their movement men of
the calibre of Mr. Hazelton.”

Thus swaggered the ZIrish Vintner and Grocer in
March, an “TIrish ”’ paper that we had occasion te pull
up some months back as a puffer of British Hop Bitters.

Now, Mr. Hazelton is a young Irishman with a future
before him, and it would be well for Ireland if there
were a great many more of his kind in evidence in public
life. He has character and brains, and he can, we aic
confident, take the rough handling that a public man
has inevitably to face as coolly and good-humouredly as
another man. But when this “Zrish” TFintner and
Grocer attacks him as a corner-boy might, that is another
question.  Mr. Hazelton very properly went to his
solicitor, and the “ Irish ’’ Vintner and Grocer, conclud-
ing that discretion was the better part of valour,
promptly ate its brave swaggering words.  Sir John
Talstaff, of March, was thrown aside; and in April the
“Irish " Vintner and Grocer went down on its marrow-
bones to Mr. Hazelton. Here is how our polite
contemporary backs down before Mr. Hazelton:—
“In my notes last month I had a paragraph in
reference to Mr. Hazelton, of Blackrock, and the clesing
of Publichouses on St. Patrick’s Day. Mr. Hazelton
has taken objection to my remarks regarding him, and
through his solicitor, Mr. John Gore, has demanded an
apology. This I now publicly tender to him, for T find
that I was altogether wrong in what I stated, and on
again looking at the paragraph in question I am sorry to
see that an unjust inference to Mr. Hazelton could be
drawn from it, namely, that he does nothing for a living,
and has a holiday all the year round. I now learn that,
far from this being the case, he occupies an important
position in the office of John E. Kent, Esq., 25 Eustace
Street, Dublin, and far from being ‘an irresponsible
chap,” as I stated, he occupies a prominent position in
the public life of County Dublin, being Deputy Vice-
Chairman of the Blackrock Urban District Council, and
a member of the Rathdown Board of Guardians, besides
having been Chairman of the original National Holiday
Committee in Dublin, two years ago. I, therefore, un-

APRIL 15, 1905. 
1·eservedly withdraw the allegations made against Mr. 
Hazelton, and again tender him my apology.'' .Per-
haps, it would be cruel to add anything to this 
humiliating crawl down on the part of this paper 
that styles itself" T'/ie Irish Vintner and Grocer." That 
cult~ll"ed organ o_f opinion had better be more care-
ful m future. 
The swagger, language and manners of 
the publichouse may be dangerous when reproduced in 
public print. 
One of the presiding geniuses of the Great Sourface 
Railway some time back dumped a.n importation from 
Great Britain upon these shores. 
Needless to say the 
dump was " saved " he also had au " accent." He was 
brought ove1· here, we believe, in order to show some of 
the mere Irish officials of the Great Sourface Railway 
how 
to 
do 
their 
business. 
He 
was 
shifted 
about from post to pillar, 
and has 
now 
been 
shifted 
back 
to 
his 
native 
Britain 
under 
cir-
cumstances 
which 
are 
not 
devoid 
of 
humour. 
The Great Sourface Railway have embarked on an ad-
vertising enterprise in Lancashire, where the trippers 
come from. 
A firm by the name of Hilliard, of Kil-
larney-needless to say, the firm is "saved "-were 
charged with the work of supplying a horse, a side-car, 
and a driver, to further on this great advertising idea 
that was conceived in the brain of one of the Great 
Sourface officials. 
We do not know whether or not the 
driver of the material manifestation of thi~ great idea 
is " saved " or not; and the nationality of the honest 
horse is . also unknown to us. 
A "poster " a:dorns the 
side-car, and the poster is a reproduction of a picture by 
a Frenchman who was imported for the job, and the 
reproduction was carried out in France. 
Whether all 
this great nation-making enterprise was carried out 
under the guidance of the conquering brain of 
Napoleon Dent, an importation who draws £2,000 
a year out .of the revenue of the Great Sour-
face Railway, or whether it was a man by the name 
of Smith who looked after the details, we cannot say; 
but the dump to whom we referred in the beginning of 
this note has been drafted back to happy England, and 
we understand that his duties are to distribute bills in 
Lancashire in connection with the tour through that 
shire of the side-car adorned by the French pictme. 
He was brought over to show the mere Irish clerks how 
to do their work; now he is drafted back to England-
and probably he will be accepted by the Lancashire 
trippers as a native specimen of the Gap of Dunloe ! 
Messrs. Richardson and Fletcher, Manure Manufac-
turers, Dublin, invite suggestions for their 190•6· Alma-
nac. 
The subject must be taken from au Irish source. 
A prize of £5 will be awarded for the best suggestion. 
Intending competitors can obtain full particulars from 
local agents or direct from the firm in Dublin. 
The Dublin Industrial Development Association is 
being wrought into shape, and there is reason to hope 
that it will do as much for Dublin manufacturers as 
the Cork Association did for those of Corle. 
Mr. George 
Perry presided at the last Council meeting, and prac-
tical men like John Irwin, T.O., John Mulligan, Alder-
man Reigh, and the like were present, and we note 
that Mr. Robert Woods, of Williams and Woods, the 
, 
well-known Dublin jam manufacturers, and Mr. J . P. 
Barrington, of the famous soap firm, and Mr. W . E. 
Shackleton, of Lucan, were co-opted on the Council. 
These class of men must take a very prominent, if not a 
controlling part in the Industries Development Associa-
tion, if it is _to be a success, and we are glad to see that 
so many of this class are interesting themselves in it. 
Mr. McVeagh's Return, if we may style it so, is up-
setting the Ascendancy very much. 
That, of course, 
is quite natural. 
Mr. Moore wanted ,to know if officials 
of the Government Departments in Ireland were free to 
decline to reveal the places where they were educated, 
and Mr .. Long replied that he was advised that the 
Government had no legal right to demand an answer 
to the que11tion. 
Now this is all humbug. 
The 
Gov,1rnment ha11 passed the 01·der for this Enquiry, and 
surely £he Imperial Parliament havo a legal right to ask 
any of its salaried servants wher~ _he wa,s educated. Was 
it from Mr. Campbell, the Sohmtor-General, that . the 
legal opinion was received 1 If Mr. Long, or the head 
of any Public Department has no power ~o com~el 
answers to these questions, su~·ely the Imperia.1 Parl!a-
·meut has power. 
Mr. Long, 1t appea1·s from a Parlia-
mentary press report, indignantly replied; "No, Sir, 
not the slightest reason," to. Captain Donelan's per-
tinent question-" Has the right ~on. gentleman any 
reason to suppose that the officials concerned are 
ashamed to give this information." 
Mr. McVeagh cer-
tainly has got the Ascendancy in a nasty predicament.

of the Co1r-oe 511ot,., takes place. 
The LEADER in-
cident is one of the items with which the Executive Com-
mittee deals. It will be remembered that at a mel!ting 
of the committee of the Cavan Branch of the Gaelic 
League it was unanimously resolved that the secretary be 
directed to enquire from the Central Executive whether 
any steps had been, or are intended to be, taken to re-
pudiate the action of the roughs concerned in the LEADER 
incident.

that 
a 
letter 
was

that 
the 
Gaelic 
League 
had 
no 
connection with the incident that occurred during 
the Language Procession in Dublin. 
Of course, that is 
not good enough. 
The League is so mixed up in it tlia.t 
it will have to expressly shake itself free of it. 
The pre-
procession resolution passed by the Dublin 
Co1r-oe 
Cec,nnc.0111 
is, at least, in a sort of continuity with the 
incident; the subsequent absurd resolution of the 
same body only added insult to injury ; the re-
marks made at a public meeting held before Lan-
guage ViTeek under the auspices of the League, and 
over which one of the Vice-Presidents presided, were at, 
least in continuity with the incident; a paper that has 
been ostentatiously styled a " confrere " by the o:fficia,J 
organ of the Gaelic League applauded the blackguards 
of the procession and endorsed their conduct; the officia.l 
organ contained no expression of regret, however formal, 
for the corner-boy episode. 
We submit that it is for 
the Co1r-oe 5not,., now to expressly denounce the 
outrage and repudiate all connection with the corner-
boys who committed it, and with those who subsequently 
endorsed it. 
Unfortunately, some of the Gaelic League 
authorities up to this have shown a lamentable lack of 
tact and diplomacy. 
For the sake of the Language 
Movement, which is above all persons and parties, we 
trust that the Co1r-oe 511ot,,at this its first meeting after 
the incident, will appreciate the gravity of the matter 
and deal with it with a strong ancl firm hand. If it be 
possible that any minority, however small, cannot see 
their way to fall in with the spirit of the protests, speci-
mens of which we have been publishing, we hope that 
the members of such a minority will give the Irish-
Ireland public the benefit of their names. 
'l''here is no 
room iu the Executive body of the Gaelic League for 
anyone whose partisanship towards any political organi-
sation prevents him falling in loyally with the spirit and 
purpose of ,the Gaelic League.

‘We have received the following from Rev. P. Dren-
[ nan, C.C., Mullinavat : — Dear Sir—I have been asked
k to forward to you the enclosed subscription of £1 5s. to-
wards the funds of the Gaelic League. The amount is
very small, and the principal reason of it being so, I
understand, from the person who collected, is the recent
regrettable incident in connection with the Language
Procession. It is a very sad reflection that you, who
have been such a power in the industrial revival, should
have been made the object of such a dastardly outrage.
You may keep the enclosed sum until you are pleased
with due satisfaction from the offenders. If such is not
forthcoming you may devote same to any purpose you
deem. suitable. Should the like occur again, our small
contribution to Gaelic League will cease.”

St. Michael’s, Moor Street,
Birmingham, 4th April, 1905.

Dear Sir—I am authorised by the Father Sherlock
Branch of the Gaclic League (Birmingham), to protest
in its name against the insult offered to you in the Lan-
guage Procession, and to join in the demand that you
shall receive adequate satisfaction for the same from
the authorities of the Gaelic League. The branch con-
siders that your signal services to the Language Move-
ment, and to the cause of Irish Ireland generally, should
have been a cause for honouring you, or, at least, for
saving you from the public affiront you suffered at the
hands of members of the Gaelic League. The branch,
too, believes that it is necessary for the well-being and
dignity of the Gaelic League itself that the disgraceful
conduct at the Procession should be strongly repudiated
by those who are in a position to protect the fair name
of the Organization.—I am, yours sincerely,

J. O’HaxroN, President.

P.S.—I am writing to the same effect to the Secretary
of the Gaelic League.

Father J. J. Carroll preached a sermon in Irish, on
St. Patrick’s Day, at St. Thomas’s Church, Chicago.
After the service members of the Irish classes met in
the priest’s parlour, and Mr. Raleigh, on behalf of
the classes, addressed Father Carroll in Irish, and
Father Carroll suitably replied in the same language.
Mr Raleigh, who is a County Limerick man, left Ire-
land 56 years ago. L

‘We are pleased to be able to announce that merit cer-
tificates, in addition to money and other prizes, will be
given in all the Literary and Language Competitions at
the Munster Feis, 1905. This ought to prove a very
important innovation, and one which will, undoubtedly,
have, the effect of popularising this function especially
among the parents of the juvenile Gaelic Leaguers of
Munster. We have it on the best authority that the
parents of some of the prize-winners in the junior com-
petitions at the Munster Fois of last year—aye, and
many of the lucky grown-ups themselves—would have
much preferred certificates giving the particulars of their
success rather than the money prizes they were so fortu-
nate in securing. Now, this year all competitors will
have' a chance of gaining not only money prizes and
medals,. but merit certificates as well, and as a result we
shall. be: disappointed: if there is not very keen rivalry,
indeed; in all the Feis Competitions on the 8th and 9th
aof next. August in Killarney. Woe, understand that the
Committee have received many encouraging promises of
support from. the Gaelic League branches of Munster.
This.is as it should be, as without the cordial co-opera-
tion of, these branches the Munster Feis would lose much:
of its efficacy in the present movement for the revival
ofour native language. ST

THE DECLINE OF PLUNKETT.

IR HORACE is incorrigible ; he is the one juryman
who is shocked ab the obstinacy of the other
oloven, One would have thought that he had done the
Department with which he is connected sufficient harm
already by the ill-advised publication of his book with-
out adding new fuel to the fire that had largely ex-
pended itself. ~We feel sure that no one feels any per-
sonal resentment against such a nice-mannered gentle-
as Sir Horace Plunkett, and when he retires into private .
life ho will carry with him ‘the hearty good wishes of all
for his health, happiness, and long life.

A private gentleman who has an oblique vision and
an undue opinion of himself is a crank ; a public official
with great power placed in his hands who suffers from
liko complaints is a public danger. Personally Sir
Horace is an amiable, well-intentioned and polished gen-
tleman with theories of his own that have but a mild
interest for any outside his own immediate circle; offi-
cally he is a man wielding large patronage and con-
siderable power who published an inflammable and ill-
advised book. It is with the official book that the Irish
public have their quarrel. It is only a quibble to say
that the book is unofficial. For ourselves, we found the
book interesting, and we welcomed it. Tt was sincere, if
not deep ; it stimulated thought, and did much to clear
the air. If these be the views of a well-meaning and
liberal man like Sir Horace Plunkett, what, pray, is at
the back of the head of the Tony Traills and the various
schools of True Blues? Had the book been written by
John Smith, of Foxrock, no one would have bothered
much about it, and it would not have found a place of
honour in the window of “ The Irish Church Mission ™
shop in Great Brunswick street. But the author being
Vice-President of a large Government Department, with
great powers for good or evil, and a huge bundle of
salaried positions to distribute, the work ceased to be the
mere semi-philosophic effort of a private individual with
an ambition to publish a book, and was stamped at
once, and rightly so, as almost an official document. It
was not the literary reputation or ability of the writer,
but the official position which he held, that gave the
book importance. Had Sir Horace not been Vice-Presi-
dent of the Department the book would have been 2
drug in the market as a sixpenny pamphlet.  Siv
Horace, semi-philosopher though he be, does not, or will
not, see this distinction. It is more flattering to his
vanity—and we all, even Sir Horace, have vanity—that
he shculd consider that the storm was raised by himself
rather than by the gentleman who happened at the time
to be Vice-President of the Department. As the obsti-
nate juryman, Sir Horace, thinks most, if not all, his
adverse critics are wrong and that he is right ; and as he
is right it follows that practically all who have entered
the lists against the book have sought to overpower not
the views of Sir Horace Plunkett, but very Truth itself!
He pictures himself as an injured innocent, whose offence
was not that he had written an unjust and ill-advised.
though well-intentioned, book, but that that he had
daved to say what he thought. It is not the daring, but
the deed, however, that people object to. We do not
dispute the right of anyone to say what he thinks of us:
but if he thought that we were robbers, and said so, we
would proceed against him for slander. Was, :
M Tnerney an enemy of criticism when he made. phe Zrish -
T+mes pay up heavily for the letter of “fair comment’
which its columns harboured? Of cowse, it is true—and

That is how the old Irish speaker philosophised, in my
hearing, fifty years ago. Tbe picture of the old man
and two children was gone out of my memory, but this
famous “ New Rule” of the National Board of Educa-
tion brought it back. This new rule is a very remark-
able thing. It is one of those things now and then to be

a purely academic character reason could afford to keep
silent, but this absurdity threatens, if let, alone, to enter

with, it is the duty of us all to at once cry “ hold!” Tt

did not get the chance to object to it. It is our duty to
fling it back in the same way.

A hands of a contractor in plumbing work, gas
fittings, hardware, etc., and a contractor in a large
way for public and government institutions has to cut

an interesting question. We have been getting some
information from Mr. Joseph Boulger, of 7 Upper Liiiey
St., late 42 Mary Street, the well known Dublin
contractor. As well as being a contractor, Mr.
Boulger is a manufacturer on quite a large scale. Since
five years ago he has about doubled his manufacturing
enterprises, and now employs about 50 to 60 hands—
exclusive, of course, of travelling fitters. In this de-
partment of his business, Mr. Boulger being a business
man would not think of manufacturing any line in
which he could not profitably meet British competition.
There are some things that are turned out by the million
in Britain that he could not compete against as a manu-
facturer—so being a sensible man he leaves these lines
to the Briton. Some time ago he got a public contract
covering some years, value about £400 or £500 a year;
he got it in competition against all comers as his was
the lowest tender; almost all the manufactured articles
within the scope of that contract are, and will be, his
own manufacture. Some brass fittings for copper con-
nections he can make under the price he would have to
pay for importing them, and the fittings he makes, be-
sides being cheaper, are, he claims, also much superior
in weight and finish.

Now in the matter of what are called instantaneous
couplings for fire hose, two or three English manufac-
turers had the exclusive trade. =~ Mr. Boulger is now
manufacturing them and can beat some of the British
manufacturers to the extent of 15 per cent. in price.
Some time ago an English contractor got a contract in
Ireland involving the supply of these and other fittings.
But if the Irish contract went to a British firm the
British firm in turn went to the Irish firm, and M.
Bulger made the goods and fitted them! This firm has
the Dublin Corporation contract for the supply of
Hydrant stand pumps, and all these are made on the
premises. The firm makes—and be it borne in mind
that Mr, Boulger would not make anything that could
not stand the test of the open market—copper steamers,
fenders, corn bins, fire irons, tin ware, and all kinds of
Plumbers’ brass work. The firm have been making
their own tin ware for the past 18 or 20 years. Every
bit of the tin ware that has gone oud &f the premises has
been made on them, and we understand that in £100
of manufactured tin ware there is about £6 of tin and
£94 of labour. ;

artisans, as a rule, more intelligent and more satis-
factory on the whole than Bribish workers. He has

TFather Rushe his task has been a labour of love. In
this volume he gives an account of the foundation to-
gether with other particulars of the various monasteries,
abboys, and similar institutions, the ruins of which
still bear eloquent witness to the religious zeal and
cnergy of the various monastic orders that worked in the
cause of religion and learning in ;reland from ‘the
carliest Christian times until the period of suppression
of Henry VIIL. g

Amouygsl; the Religious Orders whose work is dealt
with we find the Benedictines, Cistercians, Dominicans,
Franciscans, Augustinians, and Carmelites. — Father
Rushe treats cach Order separately, .'m_d deals in a chro-
nological way with the various establishments founded
by each community, quoting, at the same time, the dif-
ferent authorities from whom he has obtained the infor-
mation set forth. Thus it will be seen that, as a book
of reference alone, “ A Second Thebaid ’’ must !Je par-
ticularly valuable. But, apart altogether from its use-
fulness in this respect, the antiquarian will find in this
book much that is both instructive and interesting.
Ireland, as is well-known, is studded over with ruins,
the remnants of houses famous in the days when monas-
teries were not only the centres of learning, but were
the sources of other beneficent influences that diffused
themselves all over the land. To investigate the origin
and history of such ruins is the delight of the antiquary,
and in this volume is provided a considerable amount of
information regarding the founding and early life of
hundreds of those abbeys and priories now represented
in the dilapidated structures common throughout the
country. The tourist, although he is usually regarded
as a mundane creature, may also find much in Father
Rushe’s work to introduce a new charm and interest to
his sight-sceing expeditions. Thus, what a great deal
more pleasure is experienced in viewing Muckross Abbey
when we know that this ruin was once the friary of Irre-
lagh, founded in 1430 by The MacCarthy Mor, Prince of
Desmond, and that its walls had been sanctified by the
prayers and holiness of the Franciscan Friar who, put-
ting the world behind him, had repaired to the solitude
of Irrelagh to consecrate his life to the service of his
Creator. A still further interest is added by a know-
ledge of the fact that the Friars of Muckross were
amongst the vietims of the spoliation of Henry VIII.,
and that together with being ejected from their home
several of them afterwards received the martyr’s crown.
Acquaintance with such facts must undoubtedly in-
crease the pleasure of our visits to the remains of the
old monasteries and abbeys, and Father Rushe gives us
information similar to that relating to Muckross about,
many other ruins so frequently the objective point of
the tourist.

It very frequently occurs that a great, decisive
triumph in any cause has the effect of throwing almost
altogether into obscurity the less important victories
achieved in the same cause. As a natural consequence
the reputations of the victors in the minor struggles are
dimmed to insignificance before the lustre that History
places round the name of him who is the conqueror in
the main, deciding conflict. ~ Thus we find that to
many, Palladius is unknown, and historians dismiss
him with the scantiest references; whereas St. Patrick
is, as it were, a kind of living personality amongst us,
and volume after volume. has been filled by his
biographers. No one, of course, is prompted to deny
that all the honour paid to Ireland’s Apostle is a de-
served tribute; but it is possible to sing every praise to
Patrick and still maintain that Palladius does not
usually receive all the credit to which he is entitled.
After all, he implanted the Faith in Ireland and pre-
pared the way for his successor. He also succeeded so
far as to establish churches as, in the Scholiast on St.
Fiacc’s Hymn, we are told that Palladius “founded
some churches, namely, Teach na Roman, Killfine and
others.”” But it is perhaps in the domain of monas-

BUNG AND HIS BOND SLAYE.

Under the above heading in a recent LEADER
(March 25th), your contributor “@ipc ” deals with a
subject which to my mind is of primary national im-
portance.

Mr. Bung, “Green patriot and philanthropist,” is
ever ready to traffic in his countrys’ honour. A large
percentage of the drunkery owners of Dublin sponged
on St. Patrick this year, and will continue to do so
until they are compelled by Act of Parliament or the
terrible force of sound public opinion to do honour to
Ireland’s National Apostle, by keeping their drunkeries
closed on the 17th March.

Mr. Bung is not a philanthropist, and need not hold
forth the plea of public convenience, he has no thought
for the public; there is too much egotism in him for
that. This then is an opportune time to lay the Dublin
publican—who twned a deaf ear to the Archbishop’s
advice—on the table of public criticism. The story
of a “Bar-boy” dealt with in your columns, is by no
means an isolated instance, public attention should be
riveted on the apprentice question, because it involves
the future of a very large number of owr young men.

Young men from fowrteen to seventeen years of age
are sent by their parents up to Dublin to serve their
time to the “trade” for a period of two or three years.
In some cases a fee is demanded, and, of course, paid
by the simple parents because they are under the im-
pression that they are doing their part to make their
son a ‘“gentleman,” when in reality they are only
tightening the slave-master’s hold on the slave. Well,
the young aspirant accepts the yoke, and the mill turns.
He immediately discovers that he really is a slave, not
an apprentice to an approved trade. His first few days
in the Bungery are killing, he rises at 6-45 a.m. or 7
the latest, and his slavery begins. His ears are
offended with the foul language that greels them, he
wonders how God allows those men and women to
exist who blaspheme His name with their every breath,
but as time goes on to this he becomes accustomed, and
